quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 4 Quiz 5
quiz.subtitle: Proving Congruence Using Three Sides or Two Sides and the Included Angle
quiz.subject: Geometry
quiz.topic: Geometry: 4.5 Triangle Congruence: SSS and SAS

questions.question: The SSS Congruence Postulate states that two triangles are congruent if:
options: Two sides and the included angle of one triangle are congruent to two sides and the included angle of the other
Three sides of one triangle are congruent to three sides of the other triangle
Two angles and the included side of one triangle are congruent to two angles and the included side of the other
Three angles of one triangle are congruent to three angles of the other
questions.explanation: SSS (Side-Side-Side) states that if all three sides of one triangle are congruent to all three sides of another triangle, the triangles are congruent.

questions.question: The SAS Congruence Postulate requires which of the following to prove two triangles congruent?
options: Two sides and any angle of one triangle are congruent to the corresponding parts of the other
Two sides and the included angle of one triangle are congruent to the corresponding parts of the other
Two angles and the included side of one triangle are congruent to the corresponding parts of the other
One side and two angles of one triangle are congruent to the corresponding parts of the other
questions.explanation: SAS (Side-Angle-Side) requires two sides and the included angle — the angle formed between those two sides — to be congruent to the corresponding parts of the other triangle.

questions.question: In $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle DEF$, $AB = DE$, $BC = EF$, and $\angle B = \angle E$. Which postulate proves the triangles are congruent?
questions.explanation: Two sides ($AB = DE$ and $BC = EF$) and the included angle ($\angle B = \angle E$) are given. The angle $\angle B$ is formed by sides $AB$ and $BC$, so SAS applies.

questions.question: In $\triangle PQR$ and $\triangle STU$, $PQ = ST$, $QR = TU$, and $PR = SU$. Which postulate proves the triangles are congruent?
questions.explanation: All three pairs of corresponding sides are given as congruent: $PQ = ST$, $QR = TU$, and $PR = SU$. This satisfies the SSS Congruence Postulate.

questions.question: Why is SSA not a valid triangle congruence postulate?
options: SSA can produce two different triangles satisfying the same conditions
SSA only works for right triangles under a strict perpendicular model
SSA requires more structural information than a standard SSS postulate
SSA is the same as SAS when calculating corresponding parts in a system
questions.explanation: SSA (two sides and a non-included angle) is ambiguous because it can produce two different triangles. The given angle may swing to meet the third side at two different points, creating an ambiguous case.

questions.question: $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle DCB$ share side $\overline{BC}$. If $AB = DC$ and $\angle ABC = \angle DCB$, which postulate proves $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DCB$?
options: The triangles are not necessarily congruent
questions.explanation: We have $AB = DC$ (one side), $\angle ABC = \angle DCB$ (the included angle), and $BC = CB$ by the Reflexive Property (the other side). This gives SAS: two sides and the included angle.

questions.question: $M$ is the midpoint of both $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$. Which postulate proves $\triangle ACM \cong \triangle BDM$?
questions.explanation: Since $M$ is the midpoint of both segments, $AM = MB$ and $CM = MD$. The vertical angles $\angle AMC = \angle BMD$ are the included angles between these pairs of congruent sides. This satisfies SAS.

questions.question: In $\triangle XYZ$ and $\triangle VWX$, $XY = VW = 5$, $YZ = WX = 12$, and $XZ = VX = 13$. Are the triangles congruent?
options: No, the sides do not match in the correct order
questions.explanation: All three sides are $5$, $12$, and $13$ in both triangles. The specific vertex correspondence does not affect SSS — as long as all three sides are congruent, the triangles are congruent by SSS.

questions.question: Which of the following is sufficient to prove two triangles congruent?
options: Two sides of one triangle are congruent to two sides of the other in a plane
Three angles of one triangle are congruent to three angles of the other on a grid
Two sides and the included angle of one triangle are congruent to the corresponding parts of the other
Two angles of one triangle are congruent to two angles of the other in a plane
questions.explanation: SAS is a valid congruence postulate. AAA only guarantees similarity, not congruence. Two sides alone (SS) or two angles alone (AA) are insufficient without additional information.

questions.question: In a proof, a student writes: '$AB = DE$, $BC = EF$, $AC = DF$, therefore $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF$.' Which postulate justifies this conclusion?
questions.explanation: Three pairs of congruent sides are given: $AB = DE$, $BC = EF$, and $AC = DF$. This is a direct application of the SSS Congruence Postulate.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered SSS and SAS and can identify when each postulate applies in a geometric proof.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the postulates but review the difference between the included angle in SAS and the non-included angle in the invalid SSA case.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: SSS requires three pairs of congruent sides; SAS requires two pairs of congruent sides and the included angle; SSA does not prove congruence.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! SSS means all three sides match. SAS means two sides and the angle between them match. The included angle is the angle formed by the two sides you are using.
